title: Walden, and On The Duty Of Civil Disobedience
author: Thoreau, Henry David
summary: In the opening of Walden Thoreau recounts living alone in a self-built cabin on Walden Pond, earning his living by manual labor and observing his neighbors’ lives. He critiques the needless burdens, inherited encumbrances, and blind obedience that turn men into machines, arguing for simplicity, self-reliance, and the preservation of life’s finer fruits. He contrasts frantic, debt-driven industry with a life tested by simple experiments, questions received wisdom, and invokes myth, scripture, and nature to urge readers to examine prejudices, cherish leisure for thought, and adopt new deeds befitting new people.

characters.name: Henry David Thoreau
characters.age: 30s (author states he has lived some thirty years)
characters.personality: reflective, individualistic, critical of social conventions, advocate of simplicity
characters.lifespan: Narrator who lived two years and two months alone at Walden Pond before returning to civilized life (as described)

characters.name: townsmen / neighbors
characters.personality: busy, burdened by inherited property and conventional labor
characters.lifespan: Portrayed as leading lives of quiet desperation; no individual fate detailed

characters.name: young men (inheritors)
characters.personality: overloaded with inherited farms and responsibilities
characters.lifespan: Depicted as constrained by property and obligations; specific fates not given

characters.name: a farmer (speaker about diet)
characters.personality: practical, conventional, believes animal food is necessary for bones
characters.lifespan: Used as an example of conventional belief; no personal fate described

characters.name: teamster
characters.personality: dutiful, occupied with caring for horses and market duties
characters.lifespan: Representative laborer enslaved by opinion and work; no individual fate given

characters.name: Squire Make-a-stir
characters.personality: symbolic of local interest or enterprise driving others
characters.lifespan: Mentioned as a figure for whom others toil; fictional/representative, no personal fate

characters.name: Wilberforce
characters.personality: evoked as an emblem of reform (antislavery/advocacy) in passing
characters.lifespan: Referenced historically; not a participant in narrative

characters.name: Brahmins
characters.personality: invoked as exotic exemplars of extreme penance
characters.lifespan: Referenced as cultural examples, not characters with fates in the text

characters.name: Sandwich Islanders
characters.personality: referenced as distant peoples in comparison
characters.lifespan: Mentioned illustratively; no narrative fate given

characters.name: Iolas
characters.lifespan: Mythological reference; no fate in book

characters.name: Raleigh
characters.lifespan: Referenced historically; not part of narrative

characters.name: the ladies of the land
characters.personality: portrayed as concerned with appearances and future provision
characters.lifespan: Collective example in critique of social habits; no individual fates

characters.name: minks and muskrats
characters.personality: wild creatures used to contrast human desperation with animal bravado
characters.lifespan: Natural inhabitants of the region; mentioned illustratively

characters.name: Solomon, Roman prætors, Hippocrates, Evelyn
characters.personality: invoked as authorities or examples from history and literature
characters.lifespan: Historical figures cited; not characters in the narrative

unusual_words.definition: to force upon others; impose

unusual_words.definition: a temporary resident

unusual_words.definition: excessive reference to oneself
